site.name: Al-Fateem Academy
site.description: Al-Fateem Academy is a web development institute in Karachi training full-stack developers since 2008. Learn HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript, TypeScript, React, Next.js, PHP, Laravel, WordPress and AI automation with hands-on, job-focused instruction.

address.street: Office #91, Falak Corporate City, Near Pakistan Post Office

posts.title: How To Secure Your WordPress Website From Hackers
posts.excerpt: WordPress powers a huge share of the web, which makes it a standing target. A practical walkthrough of the hardening steps that actually matter.

body.text: WordPress is a CMS — a content management system. It is free and open source, and it is used by millions of people. WordPress works well for a wide range of sites: whether you are using it for blogging or for an eCommerce business, it is an ideal platform. Al-Fateem Academy offers a WordPress development course in Karachi that covers preventing the hacks described below.
How to secure WordPress

items: Choose strong passwords. An attacker will go for the admin login first, so avoid reusing a password from any other site. Pick something long that cannot be guessed or found in a breach dump.
Keep your WordPress core, themes and plugins up to date. This alone closes the majority of the openings attackers rely on.

Restrict user access. WordPress sites often accumulate accounts, especially when you work with a group — and every extra account is another password that might be weak. Give each user only the access their role needs, and remove accounts you no longer use.
Put a firewall in front of the site. With a firewall in place, even a delayed update is less likely to be exploited immediately.
Set up automated backups. A backup does not prevent a compromise, but it is the difference between an afternoon of restoring and losing the site.

body.text: What people build with WordPress
Blogging
Many people use online platforms to share their ideas and thoughts. Blogging is the clearest example — a WordPress site used to publish written content, pictures and video to an online audience.
Business websites
With an online store you can sell and buy directly. Businesses use WordPress to create the content that attracts customers, displaying and promoting the features of their products.

posts.title: Defining Default Password Validation Rules in Laravel
posts.excerpt: Laravel lets you define password rules once and apply them everywhere. Here is how to set a sane default across your whole application.

body.text: The Password validation rule object in Laravel 8.43 lets you set default password rules that can be used across your application. Defining them once in a service provider centralises the password validation behaviour you expect, rather than repeating the same chain of rules at every call site.
Define the defaults in a service provider
Use them in a validator
Once the defaults are registered, Password::defaults() returns them anywhere you validate:

The password rule gives you practical conventions for enforcing strong passwords, including uncompromised(), which checks the password against known data breaches so a password that has already leaked is rejected outright.

posts.title: Google Analytics Integration In WooCommerce
posts.excerpt: Connect WooCommerce to Google Analytics properly, so your revenue, funnel and product data actually reconcile with your store.

body.text: Introduction

One of the most effective ways to grow an online business is to actually measure it. Connecting Google Analytics to WooCommerce turns your store from a black box into something you can reason about — what sells, what does not, and where people give up.
In this post, Al-Fateem Academy walks through everything needed to integrate Google Analytics with WooCommerce.
What this covers

items: Plugin installation
Plugin configuration — global site tag, standard tracking, advertising network, enhanced link attribution, anonymising IP addresses, 404 tracking, transactions, and add-to-cart
Conversion tracking in Google Analytics

body.text: 1. Plugin installation

Installation is the first step. Prefer plugins built by WooCommerce itself, or ones WooCommerce explicitly recommends — they track the platform’s own changes and break less often. The WooCommerce Google Analytics Integration plugin is the obvious starting point.

items: Open your WordPress admin dashboard.
Go to Plugins.
Click Add New.
Search for your plugin.
Install and activate it.

body.text: A message appears above the plugin list once it is active. Follow it and installation is done.
2. Plugin configuration
Click "Connect WooCommerce to Google Analytics" to go straight to the plugin settings, then work through the following.

items: Get your Google Analytics ID from the Analytics home dashboard.
On the dashboard, click Admin in the bottom left-hand corner.
Add a new account or property, depending on how your Analytics is structured.
Name the account and create a new property.
Choose Web — not iOS or Android, which are for app tracking. WooCommerce is a web app.
Add your website URL.
Copy the tracking ID into the plugin settings in your WordPress admin.
Check the "Enable standard tracking" box.
Enable Enhanced Ecommerce for deeper reports.
Save settings.

body.text: Your store is now connected. Several options are set by default during that process, and they are worth understanding rather than leaving alone.
Global site tag
Enables the global site tag. The gtag.js framework then powers both Google Analytics 4 and Universal Analytics tracking.
Standard tracking
Check this if you are not already using another plugin to add standard Google Analytics. Two plugins both injecting Analytics will double-count everything.
Advertising network
Check this if you run Google Ads and want to track results. It reports through the Analytics side as well as the Ads dashboard.
Enhanced link attribution

Leave this unchecked if you are a beginner. It improves in-page analytics reports by distinguishing between multiple links to the same URL on one page, using link element IDs — but reading the output requires a deeper understanding of Analytics.
Anonymising IP addresses

Mandatory in some countries for privacy reasons. Analytics zeroes the last octet of IPv4 addresses and the last 80 bits of IPv6 addresses in memory, immediately after they reach the collection network.
404 error tracking

Tracks the error pages your visitors hit. This is the cheapest way to find broken and dead links — particularly useful after you delete a product and want to know whether people are still trying to reach it, so you can 301 them to a similar product or category.
Transactions

Check this to track transactions, revenue, refunds, shipping, tax and other financial metrics. Note that WooCommerce needs a supported payment gateway add-on — PayPal Checkout, Stripe, Square, Braintree and similar. Without a gateway, transactions are not tracked at all.
Add to cart
Fires an event whenever a visitor adds a product to the cart. This is what makes drop-off analysis possible — you can see where in the funnel people stop.
3. Conversion tracking
The two metrics worth following closely are conversion rate and drop-off pages, and neither requires going deep into Analytics.
Once your account settings are in place, create a specific event: look for Conversions in the left-hand sidebar and define it there.
Drop-off pages are the pages a visitor reached without making a purchase — also known as checkout behaviour analysis. Enabling Enhanced Ecommerce gives you the drop-off rate directly.
